questions.question: Explain the two levels of disk formatting. What is done at each level?
keyPoints: Low-level (physical) formatting: divides disk into sectors with header, data, and ECC fields
Done at the factory by the manufacturer
Logical (high-level) formatting: writes file system structures onto a partition
Includes superblock, inode table/FAT, root directory, free-space data structures
A partition can also be used as raw disk (no file system) for swap or database use

questions.question: Describe the boot process from power-on to OS kernel loading. What is the role of the MBR?
keyPoints: CPU executes firmware (BIOS/UEFI) from ROM
Firmware reads MBR from sector 0 of boot disk
MBR contains bootstrap code (446 bytes), partition table (64 bytes), magic number (2 bytes)
Bootstrap code identifies the active partition
Loads the boot block from the active partition
Boot block loads the full OS kernel into memory
Kernel initializes system and starts user processes

questions.question: Explain sector sparing and sector slipping as bad block recovery techniques.
keyPoints: Sector sparing: controller remaps bad sector to a spare from a reserved pool
Transparent to the OS — OS sees continuous sector numbers
Disadvantage: spare may be physically far, breaking sequential access
Sector slipping: controller shifts adjacent sectors by one to fill the gap
Preserves sequential access order but requires moving data of multiple sectors

questions.question: Compare the two approaches for swap-space management: raw partition vs file system swap. Which is preferred and why?
keyPoints: Raw partition: OS manages swap directly, no file system overhead, faster I/O
Disadvantage: fixed size, harder to resize, requires dedicated partition
File system swap: swap file within an existing file system, easy to create and resize
Disadvantage: slower due to file system overhead, may suffer from external fragmentation
Raw partition is preferred for performance; used by most production systems

questions.question: Explain swap-space management in detail. How does a modern OS manage swap at the page level?
keyPoints: Swap space extends main memory when RAM is full
Modern systems swap individual pages, not entire processes
Swap space is divided into page-sized slots (e.g., 4 KB each)
A swap map (array) tracks usage: one entry per slot
Entry value 0 means free; positive value indicates number of sharing processes
Linux supports both swap partitions (mkswap, swapon) and swap files
Windows uses pagefile.sys as its swap file
